explanation: The requirement states that in a worst-case failure (largely corresponding to a fail-safe mode), the system must allow the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. In terms of the atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' directly refers to the system being in a fail-safe state, and the unimpeded ability to inhale and exhale is captured by the inspiratory valve being open ('inValveOpen') and the expiratory valve being open ('outValveOpen'). The property must state that whenever the system enters failSafeMode, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves must be open, allowing for free inhalation and exhalation. Thus, the LTL property globally requires that if 'failSafeMode' holds, then both 'inValveOpen' and 'outValveOpen' must hold as well.

The natural language requirement asks that in worst-case failures, the system should always be left in a state where the patient can inhale and exhale unimpeded. In the context of this ventilator system, the atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' is described as ensuring the inspiratory valve is closed and the out valve is open. To allow inhalation and exhalation unimpeded, typically, the inspiratory valve should not be permanently closed (otherwise, inhalation is blocked), and the expiratory valve should not be permanently closed (otherwise, exhalation is blocked). The best-matching atomic proposition for unimpeded breathing is 'outValveOpen' (expiration unimpeded); for inhalation, absence of 'inValveClose' or presence of 'inValveOpen' could be relevant, but explicit 'inValveOpen' is not listed. Given 'failSafeMode' is _explicitly defined_ as the controller's state for worst-case failure, it makes sense to specify that whenever 'failSafeMode' is active, the expiratory valve should be open (allowing exhalation), and the inspiratory valve should _not_ be closed (so the patient can also inhale). Thus, the LTL asserts: Globally, if 'failSafeMode' holds, then both 'outValveOpen' must hold and 'inValveClose' must not hold. This property directly enforces that in worst-case failure states, the patient can inhale (inspiratory valve not closed) and exhale (expiratory valve open) unimpeded.

The natural language statement requires that, in the worst-case failure mode, the system still allows the patient to inhale and exhale without obstruction. The atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' explicitly represents the system's fail-safe state during a critical failure; according to its description, this is achieved by closing the inspiratory valve and opening the expiratory valve, which aligns precisely with the requirement to not impede inhalation and exhalation. We capture this requirement by expressing that whenever the system is in failSafeMode, it is never the case that both the in (inspiratory) valve is closed and the out (expiratory) valve is not open—this would block airflow. Instead, we require that when failSafeMode is active, inValveClose and outValveOpen must be true. In LTL, we can formalize this as: globally (failSafeMode implies (inValveClose and outValveOpen)), which assures that in all steps where failSafeMode holds, the required safety condition is maintained. This is best captured by LTL template 30.

The input statement requires that in the worst-case failure (failSafeMode is active), the system allows the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. According to the atomic proposition descriptions, fail-safe mode is characterized by 'in valve closed and out valve open.' For a state to allow free breathing, the inspiratory valve should be open (inValveOpen) or the expiratory (out) valve should be open (outValveOpen) at the very least (outValveOpen is sufficient for exhalation/unimpeded exhalation). Since the description explicitly says that in fail-safe mode, the valves are set for patient safety, the property can be written so that whenever failSafeMode is active, outValveOpen must also hold. The safety property is best captured by an invariant: globally, if 'failSafeMode' then 'outValveOpen'.

The requirement states that in any worst-case (fail-safe) failure, the system must leave the patient able to inhale and exhale freely—which, based on the available atomic propositions, most closely matches the definition of 'failSafeMode' (fail-safe mode: the controller detects a severe condition that required an alarm to be raised and to bring the machine to a fail-safe mode—IN valve closed, OUT valve open). For the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded, the OUT valve must be OPEN, so we require that whenever failSafeMode is active, outValveOpen must also be true. This is a classic safety property: globally it is always the case that if failSafeMode is active, then outValveOpen is also true. This is captured by the safety LTL template: G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3), where bool_exp1 = failSafeMode and bool_exp3 = outValveOpen.

The natural language requirement states that in a worst-case failure (which is described by the failSafeMode atomic proposition: fail-safe mode with in valve closed and out valve open), the patient must be able to inhale and exhale unimpeded. That can be mapped to the system being in failSafeMode implying that the inspiratory valve is closed (inValveClose is true) and the expiratory (out) valve is open (outValveOpen is true). Thus, at all times, whenever failSafeMode occurs, it must be the case that inValveClose and outValveOpen are both true, i.e.: failSafeMode → (inValveClose & outValveOpen). This is a safety property, best captured with the template 'Globally (A implies B)' (index 30), where bool_exp1 is failSafeMode and bool_exp3 is inValveClose & outValveOpen. No duration is needed for this simple always-safety property.

The input specifies a worst-case failure scenario where the controller must ensure the patient can breathe (inhale and exhale) without impairment. The most fundamental atomic propositions describing this are 'inValveClose' (inspiratory valve closed) and 'outValveOpen' (out valve open). If the inspiratory valve is closed and the expiratory (out) valve is open, the patient can exhale and inhale passively, avoiding trapping air in the lungs. In this context, the input aligns with the definition of 'failSafeMode', whose description is: 'Fail-safe mode: the controller detects a severe condition that required an alarm to be raised and to bring the machine to a fail-safe mode (in valve closed and out valve open)'. Therefore, to express that, in all cases, whenever a worst-case (failSafeMode) happens, the system ensures the necessary valve states (inValveClose and outValveOpen), we use the template that globally requires that in 'failSafeMode', (inValveClose & outValveOpen) holds. This guarantees (for all traces) that whenever the system enters failSafeMode, it goes into the configuration that permits unimpeded breathing.

The natural language requirement is that whenever the controller is in a worst-case failure (failSafeMode), the system must be left in a state that allows the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded. The most relevant atomic proposition here is 'failSafeMode' (controller triggers fail-safe state), and 'outValveOpen' (out valve is open, allowing exhalation). While there is also 'inValveClose', indicating inspiratory valve closed, the failSafeMode description itself says 'in valve closed and out valve open.' To best formalize the requirement, we interpret 'unimpeded' as 'outValveOpen' is always true during failSafeMode (so patient can always exhale). The LTL formula states: Whenever failSafeMode holds, globally outValveOpen is true. This captures the requirement within available atomic propositions.

The requirement asks that, in the case of a worst-case failure (i.e., failSafeMode is active), the system is left in a state such that the patient can inhale and exhale unimpeded. From the atomic proposition dictionary, 'failSafeMode' indicates this worst-case failure mode, and the combination '(inValveClose & outValveOpen)' corresponds to the typical fail-safe state (see definition of 'failSafeMode'). For unimpeded breathing, the inspiratory valve should not be closed and the out valve should be open, i.e., '(!inValveClose & outValveOpen)'. Therefore, we want to say: whenever failSafeMode is active, then always ('G') it should be true that the inspiratory valve is open and the out valve is open (i.e., patient can breathe in and out). The chosen template is ID 17: 'G (bool_exp2 -> (G bool_exp3))', where bool_exp2 is 'failSafeMode' and bool_exp3 is '(!inValveClose & outValveOpen)'. This LTL property expresses that, always, if the controller enters the fail-safe mode, the system remains in a state unimpeding patient breathing, as required.

The input natural language states that in the worst-case failure (failSafeMode), the controller must ensure the patient can inhale and exhale unimpeded. The atomic propositions that correspond are failSafeMode (fail-safe mode active), inValveClose (inspiratory valve closed), and outValveOpen (out valve open). According to the fail-safe mode description, when this mode is active, the system sets inValveClose and outValveOpen. For the patient to inhale and exhale unimpeded, the inspiratory valve must not be closed (!inValveClose) and the out valve must be open (outValveOpen). Thus, when failSafeMode is active, it is required that the system always satisfies (!inValveClose & outValveOpen). This is best modeled by template 10: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), with bool_exp2 = failSafeMode and bool_exp3 = ((!inValveClose) & outValveOpen). This LTL formula guarantees that whenever the fail-safe mode is active, the inspiratory valve is not closed, and the out valve is open, thereby allowing for unimpeded inhalation and exhalation.
